title: On Singular Points in the Supercooled Stefan Problem
speaker: Max Engelstein
abstract:
The supercooled Stefan problem models how water, cooled below freezing, forms into ice. Experiments have shown that this process can exhibit nucleation (pieces of ice forming spontaneously) and that fractal-like patterns can emerge in the ice. This stands in contrast with the melting problem, in which the ice is expected to be smooth at most points and times. This was recently demonstrated rigorously in groundbreaking work by Figalli, Ros Oton and Serra. 
In joint work with Inwon Kim (UCLA) and Sebastian Munoz (UCLA), we analyze a subclass of solutions to the supercooled problem which are connected to a parabolic obstacle-type problem. For this subclass we show that some of the experimentally observed behavior can occur (such as nucleation) but that the singular set cannot be completely pathological (i.e. we give bounds on the size of the singular points).

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a signed parabolic obstacle problem
proof_stages.summary: The supercooled Stefan formulation is rewritten by integrating the temperature in time, and under the local/every-point-eventually-freezes assumption this gives the obstacle-type problem 1_t w-1 w=-1_{w>0} with w00 and w_t00, whose free boundary is the object of study.

proof_stages.label: Phenomenology and scope of the freezing model
proof_stages.summary: The talk explains that a realistic supercooled model should allow nucleation and extremely fast propagation while possibly producing fractal interfaces, and then frames the eventual-freezing formulation as a distinguished class that retains the first phenomena but will rule out fractal time slices almost everywhere.

proof_stages.label: Homogeneous blow-ups and existing stratification
proof_stages.summary: The parabolic scaling leads to two-homogeneous blow-up models, with classical work giving unique blow-ups, smoothness at flat blow-ups, and coarse spatial stratification of singular points by the kernel dimension of the quadratic blow-up.

proof_stages.label: Main good/not-so-bad/rare decomposition
proof_stages.summary: Using parabolic dimension, the main theorem decomposes the free boundary into a smooth good part, a not-so-bad singular part of parabolic dimension less than n-1, and a rare part absent on almost every time slice, with a global dichotomy for whole-space solutions and comparison to the better-behaved melting-sign theorem of Figalli�1Ros-Oton�1Serra.

proof_stages.label: Cleaning lemma and reduction to two hard tasks
proof_stages.summary: A geometric measure-theoretic cleaning lemma upgrades bounds on spatial projections to parabolic-dimensional bounds when points avoid backward parabolas, so the proof is reduced to improving the projection dimension of the 0t^+1 stratum and ruling out stationary codimension-one singular blow-ups.

proof_stages.label: Exclusion of stationary codimension-one blow-ups
proof_stages.summary: The stationary blow-ups with codimension-one kernel are shown not to occur for the freezing sign by taking a second blow-up and deriving incompatible algebraic convexity/concavity constraints from the sign conditions.

proof_stages.label: Second blow-up analysis over the 0t^+1 stratum
proof_stages.summary: After subtracting the 0t^+1 profile and normalizing, finite-order second blow-ups yield homogeneous caloric polynomials handled by concavity, flat-slab estimates, and harmonic-polynomial zero-set inequalities, while infinite-order points form the rare set and are controlled by monotonicity/bootstrapping arguments and the whole-space dichotomy.
